topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo in the sudden silence of my apartment. The subject line was innocuous enough, but the content—a formal notification that my piece on urban gardening had been published, complete with a byline that was entirely unfamiliar—made my hands tremble. I clicked it open, my breath catching in my throat as my eyes scanned the text. The prose was mine. Every carefully chosen metaphor, every nuanced observation about soil composition and sunlight, was there. But the name attached to it belonged to a stranger. I reread the opening paragraph, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was a dizzying, almost unbelievable realization. I sat back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, staring blankly at the screen. My fingers began to trace patterns on the keyboard, a nervous habit, as I tried to reconcile the reality unfolding before me with the memory of my own labor.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up, I nearly dropped my coffee mug. It slid across the wooden desk with a soft clatter, but I didn't notice. My gaze was locked on the screen, the words swimming before my eyes. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks into, the one that felt like a genuine articulation of my soul. Yet, the attribution was wrong. A different name stood where mine should have been. A slow, creeping warmth began to spread through my chest, a strange, buoyant feeling that made my limbs feel light. I leaned forward, my knuckles white as I gripped the edge of the desk. I started to read again, slower this time, absorbing the text as if trying to physically pull the words back into my own grasp. A sharp intake of breath escaped me. It was impossible. The sheer audacity of the situation made my mouth go dry.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. My vision tunneled, focusing only on the text that confirmed the impossible. My entire body felt strangely still, as if suspended in mid-air. A faint tremor ran through my hands, which I slowly lowered to my lap. The ink on the page, the very structure of the argument I had constructed, was undeniably mine. But the credit—the ownership—was elsewhere. A profound sense of displacement washed over me, a dizzying shift in perspective. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet room. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside, just to look at the indifferent cityscape. My jaw hung slightly slack, and I couldn't bring myself to speak, the enormity of the discovery settling heavily in my core.
